core_meaning: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる means to show something to someone — you are actively displaying or presenting it for another person to see. Unlike <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える (to be visible, to appear), <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる always involves a deliberate agent who chooses to let someone see something.

sentences.jp: ねえ、さっき<ruby>撮<rt>と</rt></ruby>った<ruby>写真<rt>しゃしん</rt></ruby>、<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてよ。
sentences.translation: Hey, show me the photo you just took.
sentences.notes: The te-form request <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてよ is the natural casual way to ask someone to show you something — much more natural than <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてください among friends.

sentences.jp: <ruby>子<rt>こ</rt></ruby>どもたちに<ruby>手品<rt>てじな</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せたら、すごく<ruby>喜<rt>よろこ</rt></ruby>んだ。
sentences.translation: When I showed the kids a magic trick, they were really delighted.
sentences.notes: Shows the classic [<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>に<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる] pattern — the person who sees is marked with に, the thing shown with を.

sentences.jp: パスポートを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せていただけますか。
sentences.translation: Could you please show me your passport?
sentences.notes: ～ていただけますか is a polite request form commonly used by officials or service staff; <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる is the right verb here, not <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>はプレッシャーのかかる<ruby>場面<rt>ばめん</rt></ruby>でも、<ruby>落<rt>お</rt></ruby>ち<ruby>着<rt>つ</rt></ruby>いた<ruby>態度<rt>たいど</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せた。
sentences.translation: Even in high-pressure situations, she showed a composed attitude.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる can mean to display or exhibit an attitude or quality — common in written or narrative contexts, similar to English 'showed great courage.'

sentences.jp: ここで<ruby>諦<rt>あきら</rt></ruby>めるわけにはいかない。<ruby>絶対<rt>ぜったい</rt></ruby>いいところを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてやる。
sentences.translation: I can't give up here. I'll definitely show them what I'm made of.
sentences.notes: ～てやる adds a strong, determined nuance — 'I'll show you (and mean it).' This volitional use of <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる expresses resolve or proving oneself to others.

dont_use: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる requires an active agent who deliberately shows something — don't use it for things that simply appear or become visible on their own; use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える instead (e.g., <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える, not ×<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる). Also avoid confusing it with <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る (to look/watch) — <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる is always about causing someone else to see, not about seeing yourself. Don't use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる when talking about going to see a movie or show; that's <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>に<ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>く.

confused_with.contrast: A common mistake is saying ×<ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>今日<rt>きょう</rt></ruby>よく<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる when you mean the mountain is visible — <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる needs a deliberate agent doing the showing, so this sounds like Mount Fuji is choosing to display itself. Use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える for things that are (or appear to be) visible naturally: <ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>今日<rt>きょう</rt></ruby>よく<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える. Reserve <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる for when someone is actively showing something to someone else.

frequency: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる is very common in everyday spoken and written Japanese across all registers. The te-form requests (<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せて、<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてください) are especially frequent in daily conversation. The figurative sense of 'displaying a quality or attitude' is more common in written or formal speech.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる = someone actively SHOWS — remember: [<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>に<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる], and if nothing is choosing to show anything, use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える instead.
